name: Park Circle

roads: East Montague Avenue is the commercial heart of Park Circle and sees frequent pedestrian and bicycle crashes due to its mix of foot traffic, on-street parking, and vehicle throughput. The Montague Avenue and Virginia Avenue intersection is a high-collision spot where turning vehicles conflict with crosswalk traffic. Spruill Avenue running through the district carries heavy truck traffic from the adjacent industrial corridor, creating dangerous speed differentials near residential streets. The roundabout at Park Circle itself, while calming traffic, still produces sideswipe collisions from drivers unfamiliar with the yield pattern.

content_hook:
Park Circle is one of North Charleston's most vibrant and walkable neighborhoods, centered around the tree-lined circle park that gives the area its name. With the growth of restaurants, breweries, and shops along East Montague Avenue, foot and bicycle traffic has surged — but road infrastructure hasn't always kept pace.
Roden Law's Charleston office represents Park Circle residents injured in car accidents, pedestrian collisions, bicycle crashes, and slip-and-fall incidents throughout the neighborhood. Our attorneys understand the unique traffic patterns created by Park Circle's mix of residential streets, commercial corridors, and the nearby industrial areas along Spruill Avenue.
If you've been injured in Park Circle, you have rights under South Carolina law. Contact Roden Law for a free case review — we charge no fees unless we win your case.

faqs.question: What should I do after a car accident in Park Circle?
faqs.answer: Call 911, seek medical attention, document the scene with photos, exchange information with other drivers, and contact a personal injury lawyer before speaking with insurance companies. Under South Carolina law, you have 3 years to file a personal injury claim (S.C. Code § 15-3-530), but evidence is best preserved immediately.

faqs.question: Are pedestrian accidents common in Park Circle?
faqs.answer: Yes. Park Circle's walkable commercial district along East Montague Avenue generates significant pedestrian traffic. Drivers turning from side streets or pulling out of on-street parking frequently fail to yield to pedestrians, making this one of North Charleston's higher-risk areas for pedestrian collisions.

faqs.question: How much does it cost to hire Roden Law for a Park Circle injury case?
faqs.answer: Nothing upfront. Roden Law works on a contingency fee basis, meaning you pay no attorney fees unless we recover compensation for your injuries. Your initial consultation is always free.

Olde North Charleston encompasses the historic Navy Yard district, one of the Lowcountry's most ambitious redevelopment projects. The former Charleston Naval Shipyard is being transformed into a mixed-use community, but the ongoing construction and heavy vehicle traffic create elevated injury risks for workers and residents alike.
Roden Law's Charleston office represents Olde North Charleston residents and Navy Yard workers injured in construction accidents, vehicle collisions, and premises liability incidents. Our attorneys are familiar with the unique hazards of active redevelopment zones, including construction site injuries, defective road conditions, and inadequate signage.
If you've been hurt in Olde North Charleston or the Navy Yard, don't wait to get legal help. South Carolina's 3-year statute of limitations (S.C. Code § 15-3-530) begins on the date of your injury.

faqs.question: Can I file a claim for a construction injury at the Navy Yard?
faqs.answer: Yes. If you were injured at a construction site in the Navy Yard due to someone else's negligence — such as a contractor failing to maintain safe conditions — you may have a personal injury claim separate from workers' compensation. Roden Law can evaluate both options in a free consultation.

faqs.question: Who is liable for accidents caused by poor road conditions in Olde North Charleston?
faqs.answer: Liability may fall on the property owner, developer, contractor, or local government responsible for maintaining the road. In redevelopment areas like the Navy Yard, multiple parties may share responsibility. An experienced attorney can investigate and identify all liable parties.

faqs.question: What types of compensation can I recover after an injury in Olde North Charleston?
faqs.answer: You may be entitled to medical expenses, lost wages, pain and suffering, and other damages depending on your case. South Carolina uses a modified comparative fault rule, so you can recover as long as you are less than 51% at fault for the accident.

faqs.question: Who is at fault if a rental car driver hits me near the Charleston airport?
faqs.answer: The at-fault driver is typically liable regardless of whether they are driving a rental car. In some cases, the rental car company may also bear responsibility. South Carolina's modified comparative fault rule means you can recover damages as long as you are less than 51% at fault.

faqs.question: Can I sue if I'm injured in a parking lot at Tanger Outlets?
faqs.answer: Yes. Property owners have a duty to maintain safe conditions for visitors. If poor lighting, inadequate signage, potholes, or other hazards contributed to your injury, the property owner may be liable under South Carolina premises liability law.

faqs.question: How long do I have to file an injury claim in Dorchester Terrace-Waylyn?
faqs.answer: Under South Carolina law, you generally have 3 years from the date of injury to file a personal injury lawsuit (S.C. Code § 15-3-530). However, it's best to contact an attorney as soon as possible to preserve evidence and protect your rights.

faqs.question: Why is Rivers Avenue so dangerous for pedestrians in Liberty Hill?
faqs.answer: Rivers Avenue carries high-speed traffic through a densely populated residential area with limited crosswalks, narrow sidewalks, and inadequate lighting. These infrastructure deficiencies force pedestrians into dangerous crossing situations, making it one of the Lowcountry's most hazardous pedestrian corridors.

faqs.question: Can I file a claim if a government-maintained road caused my accident?
faqs.answer: Yes. Under the South Carolina Tort Claims Act, you can file claims against government entities responsible for road maintenance, though there are special notice requirements and shorter deadlines. An attorney can ensure you meet all procedural requirements.

faqs.question: What if I was partially at fault for my pedestrian accident in Liberty Hill?
faqs.answer: South Carolina follows a modified comparative fault rule. You can still recover compensation as long as you were less than 51% at fault. Your award will be reduced by your percentage of fault. For example, if you are 20% at fault, you recover 80% of your damages.

faqs.question: Who is liable for an accident on a private road in Oak Terrace Preserve?
faqs.answer: On private community roads, the HOA or property management company responsible for road maintenance may be liable for accidents caused by defective conditions like potholes, poor lighting, or missing signage. faqs.question: Can I sue my HOA if I'm injured on community property?
faqs.answer: Potentially, yes. If the HOA failed to maintain safe conditions in common areas — such as sidewalks, parking areas, or community facilities — they may be liable under South Carolina premises liability law. An attorney can review your HOA's maintenance obligations and your case facts.

faqs.question: What is the statute of limitations for a personal injury case in South Carolina?
faqs.answer: Under South Carolina law (S.C. Code § 15-3-530), you generally have 3 years from the date of injury to file a personal injury lawsuit. Some exceptions may shorten or extend this deadline, so consulting an attorney promptly is important.

faqs.answer: Claims against the federal government (including military vehicles) are governed by the Federal Tort Claims Act, which has specific procedures and deadlines different from state law. faqs.question: What should I do if I'm in a car accident near Northwoods Mall?
